food_subgroup: Mixed seafood set meal with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a prawn-and-squid Indian-style set meal; closest parent 'seafood dish' (FOODON_03315595) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (crustaceans and molluscs, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for a mixed prawn-squid set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Seafood dish' is the closest available parent concept; no specific term for prawn-sotong set meal exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal plate comprising approximately 200 g of steamed white rice, 100 g of spiced prawns, and 100 g of stir-fried squid, totalling approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked prawns and squid with rice — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp, cooked squid, and cooked white rice
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a prawn-and-sotong Indian set meal. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite: approximately 50% steamed white rice (USDA FDC #169756), 25% cooked shrimp (USDA FDC #175180), and 25% cooked squid (USDA FDC #175168), with an adjustment for cooking oil and spices consistent with ICMR-NIN data for Indian seafood preparations. Values are per 100 g of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed seafood dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a prawn-sotong Indian set meal. GI estimated at 50 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being boiled white rice (GI ~50–64 per University of Sydney GI Database; conservative lower bound used given protein and fat co-ingestion from prawns and squid, which attenuate postprandial glucose response). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 79.2) / 100 = 39.6, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 19.8 × 4 = 79.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1240 mg per set meal)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that one set meal provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion reduction or requesting less salt and sauce is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~39.6) driven by white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of approximately 39.6 per set meal is high for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion and increasing the seafood component can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from prawns and squid (~95 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns and squid contribute dietary cholesterol (~380 mg per set meal); while current evidence suggests dietary cholesterol has a modest effect on LDL in most individuals, the combined sodium and cholesterol load warrants moderation for those with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn and Sotong Set Meal is a popular Indian-style seafood set meal served in Singapore coffee shops and casual eateries such as Baba Eating House. The dish typically consists of a generous portion of steamed white rice accompanied by spiced stir-fried prawns and squid (sotong), cooked with onion, tomato, curry leaves, chilli, and oil in a style characteristic of South Indian and Indian-Muslim cuisine. The prawns are usually shell-on or peeled and cooked until just firm, while the squid is sliced into rings or scored pieces and stir-fried to a tender, slightly chewy texture. The overall flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic from the spice blend. Nutritionally, the set meal provides a moderate energy density of approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution (~9.2 g per 100 g) from the seafood components, which supply complete amino acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and iodine. The white rice base contributes the majority of carbohydrates and drives a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving. Sodium content is elevated due to seasoning and sauces, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal-certified in Singapore Indian Muslim establishments.
common_user_queries: What is prawn sotong set meal?
How many calories in prawn and squid set meal Singapore?
Is prawn sotong set meal healthy?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked shrimp, cooked squid, and steamed white rice. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled white rice, adjusted for protein-fat co-ingestion. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp and squid — no industrial trans fats are present in these seafood ingredients. 4. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this dish does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish is classified as Indian cuisine based on the input record and the restaurant context (Baba Eating House, South Bridge Road, Singapore), which serves Indian and Indian-Muslim food. 6. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 7. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard set meal plate; actual restaurant portions may vary.
